hook: Roman Nice on the hill above the modern city, baths and arena hiding in olive grass
facts: Cemenelum served as capital of the Roman province Alpes Maritimae
Three thermal bath complexes survive, plus an amphitheatre for about 5,000
The arena began in the 2nd century as a drill ground, enlarged in the 3rd
The site sits beside the archaeological museum and Matisse villa
w: Long before the seafront, Romans built their provincial capital up here on the hill: Cemenelum, with three bathhouses and an arena that drilled cohorts before it staged games for five thousand. The brick and rubble lie low in the olive grass now, edged by museums. The first Nice, quietly outranking the second.

hook: Matisse collection inside a blood-ochre 17th-century villa among the Roman ruins
facts: Housed in the Villa des Arenes, a red-ochre 17th-century Genoese-style house
Nice bought the villa in 1950; the museum opened in 1963
Holdings came largely from the artist's heirs after his death in Nice
Stands directly above the Cemenelum arena and baths
w: A blood-ochre Genoese villa, fake painted windows and all, parked among Roman bath ruins and olive trees. Matisse spent his last decades in Nice and his heirs gave the city the cut-outs, drawings and bronzes that fill it. The trompe-l'oeil facade fooled you on the way in; the colour inside does the rest.

hook: Hilltop monastery graveyard where Matisse and Dufy lie, sea beyond the cypresses
facts: Henri Matisse and Raoul Dufy are both buried here, plus Roger Martin du Gard
Matisse's grave draws flowers and letters from aspiring painters
Dufy's stone sits low and almost hidden by the gate from the garden
Adjoins a Franciscan monastery and a rose garden over the Paillon valley
w: Two painters chose this quiet ground over the hill's grander tombs. Matisse's grave collects flowers and notes from young artists; Dufy's hides small and low by the garden gate, easy to walk past. Behind, monks' rose terraces drop toward the Paillon and the far sea. Colourists at rest among the cypresses.

hook: Franciscan kitchen garden of roses and citrus on a terrace above the valley
facts: Former monks' vegetable garden and orchard of about 9,550 square metres
Keeps its original layout with a central well and rose-covered pergolas
Over a hundred rose varieties grow among olive, cypress and citrus
A lower terrace gives a panorama of the Paillon valley and sea; entry is free
w: Franciscans kept this terrace as a working kitchen garden, and it still wears the layout: central well, pergolas swallowed in climbing roses, lemon and orange trees punctuating the lawn. Over a hundred rose varieties, and a lower platform that drops the whole Paillon valley and the sea at your feet. Free, and barely visited.

hook: Purpose-built home for Chagall's Biblical Message, planned with the painter himself
facts: Built in 1973 to hold the seventeen-canvas Biblical Message cycle
Designed by Andre Hermant in close collaboration with Chagall
Paintings illustrate Genesis, Exodus and the Song of Songs
Set in an olive-planted park at the foot of boulevard de Cimiez
w: Chagall handed the state seventeen huge canvases on Genesis, Exodus and the Song of Songs, and a building rose to hold them while he watched. He helped plan the light, the wall colours, the stained glass. Few painters get a museum built to their own brief in their lifetime. Olive trees outside, blue dreaming within.

hook: Six gilded onion domes raised for the Riviera's wintering Romanov colony
facts: Largest Eastern Orthodox cathedral outside Russia, built 1903-1912
Opened in 1912 with the backing of Tsar Nicholas II
Stands where Alexander II's son died of meningitis at the Villa Bermond
Property of the Russian Federation and a French national monument
w: Six onion domes under gold leaf, dropped into a Riviera side street for the Russian aristocrats who wintered here. They raised it where a young Romanov heir had died of meningitis at the family villa, the Tsar himself helping fund it. The grandest Orthodox church beyond Russia's borders, marooned by history near the station.

hook: Fake mountain waterfall on the castle hill, an overflow tank in disguise
facts: An artificial waterfall inaugurated 27 June 1885 on the castle ruins
Fed by the new Vesubie valley aqueduct, water carried some 50 km
Doubled as the overflow for the city's first modern water supply
Sits atop the keep of a castle Louis XIV destroyed in 1706
w: It looks like an alpine cascade crashing off the hilltop, but it earns its keep: built in 1885 to mark Nice's first modern water supply, the falls double as the overflow for the reservoirs above. The water comes fifty kilometres from the Vesubie. Romantic spray over the ruins of a castle Louis XIV blew up in 1706.

hook: Christian and Jewish graveyards crowning the hill where the castle once stood
facts: Two adjoining cemeteries, Christian and Jewish, both dating from 1783
Laid out on the heights of Castle Hill after the fortress was gone
Crowded marble tombs of Nice's old families and exiles
Among the highest viewpoints over the Baie des Anges
w: Where the fortress once sat, the dead now hold the high ground: a Christian cemetery and a Jewish one side by side, both opened in 1783. Marble angels and obelisks of old Nice families and wintering exiles, packed tight on the summit. Quiet paths, sea on every side, the best view in town reserved for those past caring.

hook: Stout belvedere tower on the castle hill where Berlioz heard King Lear
facts: Built in the 19th century on a bastion of the destroyed castle
Once called the tour du Mole, then Saint-Elme's tower
Hector Berlioz reportedly began his King Lear overture nearby
Now the Bellandarium, telling the hill's thousand-year story
legend: They say Berlioz sketched the opening of King Lear within sight of this tower.
w: A squat round belvedere on the castle bastions, going by tour du Mole and Saint-Elme before settling on Bellanda. They say Berlioz, lodging below, heard the opening of his King Lear in this air. Inside, a diorama spools out the hill's thousand years. Outside, the whole Bay of Angels laid flat beneath you.

hook: Baroque palace stuffed with old instruments and a 1738 apothecary's shop
facts: A 17th-century aristocratic residence on rue Droite in the old town
Holds over 500 historic musical instruments, France's second collection
A complete 1738 pharmacy with apothecary jars and instruments survives
Owned by the Vintimille-Lascaris family until 1802; museum since 1970
w: Behind a plain door on a dark old-town lane, a Genoese baroque blast: frescoed ceilings, a monumental stair, tapestries. The Lascaris counts left it in 1802; the city filled it with five hundred old instruments and reinstalled a 1738 pharmacy, jars and all. Faded grandeur with viols and apothecary's bottles.

hook: Elliptical baroque jewel on the market square, all painted false marble
facts: Built 1740s-1770s on plans by Bernardo Vittone after Guarini
An elliptical nave with no straight lines, ringed by curved chapels
False marbles, gilded stucco and Bistolfi murals fill the interior
Tended by the order of the Black Penitents
w: A modest yellow front on the flower-market square hides one of the Riviera's great baroque interiors. Built after a Guarini design, the nave runs on ellipses, not a straight line in the plan; false marble, gilded stucco and a ceiling painted to look like open sky. Still the Black Penitents' chapel. Rarely open, worth the wait.

hook: Medieval gates and bridge found by tram-diggers, sealed in a vault below the square
facts: Found in 2007 during tramway works, 10 cm under the asphalt
About 2,000 square metres, second only to the Louvre's crypt by area
Holds the Pairoliere tower, bastion, aqueduct and the old Turin-road bridge
Opened to guided visits in October 2012
w: Diggers laying the tramline hit stone ten centimetres down and stopped: under Place Garibaldi lay Nice's medieval edge, a round tower, a bastion, an aqueduct, the bridge pillars to the Turin road. Rather than bury it again, the city roofed two thousand square metres of it. A drowned fortress reached only by guided tour.

hook: Hilltop observatory where Garnier built the walls and Eiffel floated the dome
facts: Built from 1879 on Mont Gros at about 370 m in a 35-hectare wood
Charles Garnier designed the buildings; Gustave Eiffel the great dome
The dome spans about 22.4 m inside and rests on a ring of liquid float
Still an active research site of the Cote d'Azur Observatory
w: An odd pairing on a forested ridge east of town: the man who built the Paris Opera raised these walls, and the man who built the tower floated their dome. Eiffel's hundred-tonne cupola turns on a ring of liquid so it can swing without strain. Opera grandeur and engineering trick, aimed at the stars over the bay.

hook: Belle Epoque palace of a marshal's heir, gardens fronting the sea
facts: Built 1898-1901 for Prince Victor Massena, grandson of Napoleon's marshal
Designed by Danish architect Hans-Georg Tersling in neoclassical style
Gardens laid out by landscaper Edouard Andre
Given to the city in 1919 and a museum of Nice's history since 1921
w: A marshal's great-grandson built this Italianate palace by the sea, then his line gave it to the city. Inside, the Riviera's Belle Epoque told through furniture, portraits and the bric-a-brac of grand families; outside, a formal garden by the man who landscaped half of fashionable Europe. Napoleonic blood, seaside marble.

hook: Renaissance fort with spade-shaped bastions guarding two bays at once
facts: Built 1557-1560 after the 1543 Siege of Nice, on the Boron ridge
Almost perfectly preserved, with ace-of-spades corner bastions
Stands at about 220 m between Nice and Villefranche-sur-Mer
A rare intact example of mid-16th-century military architecture
w: Up in the Boron pines sits a near-perfect Renaissance fort, raised in three years after Nice was sacked in 1543. Its corner bastions splay like aces of spades, stone sentry-boxes on each, watching the Baie des Anges one way and Villefranche the other. Four centuries on, barely a stone out of place. A war machine gone quiet.

n: Gare du Sud et Marche de la Liberation
hook: Eiffel train shed reborn as a food hall beside the city's working market
facts: Opened 1892 as terminus of the metre-gauge Chemins de Fer de Provence
Its iron shed was Eiffel's pavilion from the 1889 Paris Exposition
Closed in 1991, derelict until reopening as a food hall in 2019
Sits on the edge of the Liberation market, mornings Tuesday to Sunday
w: The glass-and-iron shed once sheltered narrow-gauge trains to the Alps, and before that stood at the 1889 Paris Expo as Eiffel's own pavilion, hauled south afterward. Trains stopped in 1991; it rotted, then reopened in 2019 packed with food stalls. Outside, the Liberation market still feeds Nice at dawn. Reused twice over.
